text: In this video I'll be taking you through how I like to perform a robotic umbilical hernia repair with mesh using the tap technique or the transabdominal preperitoneal technique. This is a mesh and sutures I'll be using for this case. Positioning is very important. I usually do use three trocars of eight millimeters in size. It's important to have a little bit of flex in the bed and also I like to push the legs slightly away from the trocars to get a little more access. in the pelvis to avoid collisions with the arm.

The surgeon uses an OptiView technique with an eight-millimeter robotic trocar to gain access to the peritoneum, achieving insufflation to fifteen millimeters of mercury. A small umbilical defect with incarcerated fat is visible. Additional trocars are placed under direct visualization, and the robotic system is docked for the procedure.

The surgeon visualizes a small umbilical hernia and uses hot scissors with electrocautery and a pro grasp to perform dissection. A wide peritoneal flap is created, approximately ten centimeters in diameter, to accommodate the mesh. Blunt dissection is employed to preserve muscle fibers and avoid injury, with traction and counter traction applied using two robotic arms. The dissection progresses near the umbilical hernia sac, ensuring the peritoneal covering remains intact over the mesh. The robotic 3D optics enhance visualization, allowing for precise dissection with minimal bleeding as the defect opens up.

The surgeon separates the skin from the hernia sac, revealing a clear defect in the fascia at the umbilicus. The hernia defect is identified, and the mesh is opened for placement. A nine-centimeter mesh is prepared for a defect measuring approximately two centimeters.

The surgeon uses a robotic pro grasp to measure anatomical structures, with the tips measuring approximately two centimeters. Sutures are passed to close the peritoneum, starting slightly away from the defect. The surgeon employs a PDS suture, known for its toughness, to close the fascial defect, reducing intra-abdominal pressure to twelve millimeters of mercury to facilitate closure. The closure is performed in multiple runs to ensure a secure approximation of the tissue.

The surgeon completes the closure of the fascial defect, ensuring no lipoma remains inside, and the assistant checks for any trapped air. The coated mesh is unrolled and positioned with the coating facing the bowel to prevent direct contact with intra-abdominal organs. Using absorbable sutures, the mesh is secured in place without tacking, utilizing a double-arm suture technique to prevent mesh migration.

The surgeon finalizes the closure of the peritoneum, securing the mesh in place by sandwiching it between the patient's peritoneum and the small bowel. Sutures are tied down and removed, completing the fixation process to minimize future adhesions.

This patient did very well, was discharged home several hours later and had a very uneventful post-operative course. Another thing we like to routinely do is place compression dressing on these patients. We just use a small piece of a four by four and a tegaderm. We do use a needle to create a vacuum here. And I think this can potentially decrease the dead space of the area and perhaps prevent a seroma. The needle is put in. And we hook it up to suction, which does create a tight vacuum seal. This dressing is taken down three days later.
